name: Dong Quai
short_description: Dong quai is the root of Angelica sinensis, a traditional botanical used in women-focused wellness formulas and herbal balance routines.
overview_subtitle: A practical guide to Angelica sinensis, traditional use, label comparison, and safety cautions.
overview_content:
Dong quai is the common name for the root of Angelica sinensis, a plant used in traditional East Asian herbal practice and often positioned in modern supplements for women-focused wellness, cycle comfort, menopause-transition support, and general vitality. It is sometimes called female ginseng, although it is not the same plant as Panax ginseng or eleuthero. The ingredient is usually sold as dried root, powdered root, liquid extract, capsule, tablet, tea, or a component of broader botanical formulas.

For Suppriva readers, the most important point is that dong quai should be evaluated as a traditional botanical, not as a hormone therapy, fertility treatment, medication replacement, or guaranteed solution for menstrual or menopausal symptoms. Supplement labels may describe the ingredient in broad wellness language, but the real comparison should focus on the root form, extract ratio, serving size, formula partners, safety cautions, and whether the product explains who should avoid it.

Dong quai contains naturally occurring compounds such as ferulic acid, ligustilide, polysaccharides, coumarin-like constituents, and other aromatic molecules. These compounds are part of why the root is discussed in relation to circulation, smooth-muscle signaling, and traditional reproductive wellness routines. However, human evidence is not strong enough to treat dong quai as a stand-alone medical intervention. It is best framed as an educational ingredient that may appear in formulas designed for comfort, transition, and herbal balance.

Safety review is especially important with dong quai. Memorial Sloan Kettering notes cautions around blood-thinning medication, pregnancy, breastfeeding, and hormone-sensitive conditions. Because some dong quai constituents may be relevant to bleeding risk or hormone-sensitive contexts, people should not add it casually when using anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, hormone therapies, or complex prescription routines. Those preparing for surgery should also discuss timing with a clinician.

Product comparison should also consider the complete formula. Dong quai is often paired with black cohosh, chaste tree berry, red clover, evening primrose oil, ginger, or other botanicals. Those combinations can create overlapping effects or additional cautions. A high-quality product should clearly identify the amount of dong quai, the plant part used, whether it is standardized, and whether the label provides practical warnings.

Dong quai has a distinctive aromatic, earthy, slightly bitter profile when used as tea or powder. Capsules can avoid taste but still require careful label review. The ingredient may be more suitable for adults seeking to understand traditional botanical options than for people looking for immediate symptom relief. It should not be used to self-treat heavy bleeding, severe pain, irregular bleeding, infertility, hot flashes, or any diagnosed condition without professional guidance.

Suppriva presents dong quai as a wellness education topic so readers can compare labels responsibly. The best approach is to treat it as one part of a broader health conversation: identify the exact product form, review medication and pregnancy cautions, avoid stacking similar formulas, and speak with a qualified healthcare professional if symptoms are persistent, severe, or medically complex.

A practical buying checklist can make dong quai easier to evaluate. Look for the Latin name Angelica sinensis, the plant part root, an honest serving size, clear directions, and warnings that match the seriousness of the ingredient. Be cautious with labels that imply hormone-balancing certainty, guaranteed cycle relief, or broad women's health claims without explaining dose and safety. A responsible product should make it easy to understand what is in the bottle and who should avoid it.
interesting_fact: Dong quai is sometimes nicknamed female ginseng, but botanically it belongs to the Angelica genus and is not a true ginseng.
how_it_works_subtitle: Dong quai is studied for aromatic root compounds, traditional use patterns, and circulation-related wellness context.
how_it_works_highlight_description: Dong quai is best evaluated by root form, formula context, dose clarity, and pregnancy, bleeding-risk, and hormone-sensitive cautions.
how_it_works_content:
Dong quai does not work like an immediate stimulant, hormone prescription, or pain reliever. Its traditional use is tied to the root's aromatic compounds, plant acids, polysaccharides, and other naturally occurring constituents. Modern supplement discussions often focus on how these compounds may relate to normal smooth-muscle, circulation, and antioxidant signaling, but these mechanisms remain educational rather than treatment claims.

Because dong quai is usually used in formulas rather than by itself, the surrounding ingredients matter. A product may combine it with chaste tree berry, black cohosh, ginger, red clover, or mineral support. The overall formula can change the safety profile and user experience. Readers should compare the entire supplement facts panel instead of judging dong quai in isolation.

The ingredient also requires special safety awareness. Its potential relevance to bleeding risk, pregnancy, breastfeeding, surgery timing, and hormone-sensitive conditions means it should be approached conservatively. People with significant symptoms should seek medical evaluation rather than relying on herbal supplements.
benefits_subtitle: Educational benefits commonly discussed for dong quai in traditional botanical and women's wellness formulas.

benefits_json.title: Women's Wellness Context
benefits_json.description: Dong quai appears in formulas marketed around cycle comfort, transition support, and general reproductive wellness education.

benefits_json.title: Circulation-Related Tradition
benefits_json.description: Traditional use often connects dong quai with blood-flow and vitality concepts, though modern users should avoid disease-treatment assumptions.

benefits_json.title: Botanical Formula Support
benefits_json.description: It is commonly paired with other herbs, making it useful for learning how multi-herb women's formulas are built.

benefits_json.title: Label Safety Awareness
benefits_json.description: Dong quai encourages careful review of pregnancy, surgery, bleeding-risk, and hormone-sensitive condition cautions.

benefits_json.title: Traditional Herb Education
benefits_json.description: The ingredient helps readers distinguish historical use from modern evidence and responsible supplement comparison.

benefits_json.title: Aromatic Root Profile
benefits_json.description: Dong quai's distinctive root chemistry gives it a recognizable herbal profile in capsules, tinctures, and teas.

dosage_subtitle: Serving size varies widely by root powder, extract ratio, tea, tincture, and combination formula.
typical_dose: Many dong quai products provide several hundred milligrams of root powder or extract per serving, but users should follow the finished product label.
dosage: Many dong quai products provide several hundred milligrams of root powder or extract per serving, but users should follow the finished product label.
dosage_content:
Dong quai dosage cannot be judged by milligrams alone because labels may use powdered root, concentrated extract, tea, tincture, or a multi-herb blend. A concentrated extract may not be equivalent to a raw-root capsule. The most responsible approach is to follow the specific product label and avoid combining multiple dong quai-containing products.

People taking blood thinners, antiplatelet medication, hormone-related therapies, or medications for complex conditions should ask a healthcare professional before use. Dong quai should generally be avoided during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless a qualified clinician provides individualized guidance.
safety_subtitle: Dong quai requires careful review for pregnancy, breastfeeding, bleeding risk, surgery timing, and hormone-sensitive conditions.

side_effects_json.title: Digestive Upset
side_effects_json.description: Some users may notice nausea, diarrhea, bloating, appetite changes, or stomach discomfort.

side_effects_json.title: Photosensitivity
side_effects_json.description: Dong quai may increase sun sensitivity in some people, especially at higher exposure or with certain medications.

side_effects_json.title: Bleeding-Risk Concern
side_effects_json.description: People using blood-thinning medication or preparing for surgery should review dong quai with a clinician.

side_effects_json.title: Hormone-Sensitive Caution
side_effects_json.description: People with hormone-sensitive conditions should avoid self-directed use unless medically advised.

drug_interactions_json: Anticoagulant and antiplatelet medications, including warfarin, should be reviewed because dong quai may increase bleeding-risk concerns.
NSAIDs and other products that can affect bleeding should be discussed before combining with dong quai.
Hormone therapies, oral contraceptives, or treatments for hormone-sensitive conditions should not be combined without professional guidance.
Photosensitizing medications may require extra caution because dong quai may increase sun sensitivity in some users.
who_should_avoid_json: Pregnant individuals unless specifically advised by a qualified clinician.
Breastfeeding individuals unless professional guidance confirms appropriateness.
People with hormone-sensitive cancers or hormone-sensitive medical conditions.
People taking blood thinners or scheduled for surgery without clinician clearance.
Anyone using dong quai instead of medical evaluation for abnormal bleeding, severe pain, or persistent symptoms.
food_sources_subtitle: Dong quai is primarily used as an herbal root rather than a common food ingredient.
food_sources_content: Dong quai is not typically consumed as an everyday food. It is more often used as dried root, tea, powder, tincture, capsule, or extract in herbal products. Food exposure and supplement exposure should not be treated as equivalent.

food_sources_json.title: Dried Angelica Root
food_sources_json.description: Traditional preparations may use sliced or dried Angelica sinensis root.

food_sources_json.title: Herbal Tea
food_sources_json.description: Some products use dong quai root in tea blends, though dose can vary considerably.

food_sources_json.title: Capsules and Extracts
food_sources_json.description: Modern supplements commonly use capsules, tablets, tinctures, or liquid extracts.

faq_subtitle: Common questions about Dong Quai, supplement labels, dosage, safety, and practical comparison.

faq_json.question: What is dong quai?
faq_json.answer: Dong quai is the root of Angelica sinensis, a traditional botanical used in herbal wellness formulas, especially women's wellness blends.

faq_json.question: Is dong quai the same as ginseng?
faq_json.answer: No. It is sometimes called female ginseng, but it is not Panax ginseng and belongs to a different botanical group.

faq_json.question: What is dong quai commonly used for?
faq_json.answer: It is commonly discussed in cycle comfort, menopause-transition, circulation-related, and traditional botanical wellness formulas.

faq_json.question: Can dong quai replace hormone therapy?
faq_json.answer: No. Dong quai is not a hormone therapy and should not replace prescribed treatment or medical evaluation.

faq_json.question: Is dong quai safe during pregnancy?
faq_json.answer: Dong quai is generally avoided during pregnancy unless a qualified healthcare professional specifically advises otherwise.

faq_json.question: Can dong quai interact with blood thinners?
faq_json.answer: Yes. People taking anticoagulant or antiplatelet medication should speak with a healthcare professional before using dong quai.

faq_json.question: What are possible dong quai side effects?
faq_json.answer: Possible side effects include digestive upset, diarrhea, appetite changes, photosensitivity, and bleeding-risk concerns in susceptible users.

faq_json.question: How should dong quai supplements be compared?
faq_json.answer: Compare root form, extract ratio, serving size, formula partners, quality testing, and safety warnings.

faq_json.question: Can dong quai be taken with other women's wellness herbs?
faq_json.answer: Combination formulas are common, but stacking herbs may increase cautions, so the full ingredient panel should be reviewed.

faq_json.question: Who should avoid dong quai?
faq_json.answer: Pregnant or breastfeeding individuals, people with hormone-sensitive conditions, and people using blood thinners should avoid self-directed use.

sidebar_profile_content: Dong quai is an Angelica sinensis root ingredient used in traditional women's wellness formulas. It should be reviewed carefully for pregnancy, bleeding-risk, surgery, and hormone-sensitive cautions.
sidebar_at_a_glance_content: Dong quai is best understood as a traditional botanical for education, not a treatment. Review dose form, formula partners, blood-thinner cautions, pregnancy guidance, and hormone-sensitive context before use.

research_subtitle: A balanced interpretation of available supplement research.
research_content:
Dong quai research includes traditional-use reviews, laboratory work on root constituents, and limited human studies. Evidence is not strong enough to use it as a stand-alone treatment for menstrual, menopausal, or hormone-related concerns.

Safety references emphasize caution with pregnancy, breastfeeding, anticoagulant medication, surgery timing, and hormone-sensitive conditions. Product quality and formula context also affect real-world use.

research_json.title: Evidence Varies by Extract
research_json.description: Research findings depend on the exact species, preparation, dose, study design, and population being evaluated.

research_json.title: Label Context Matters
research_json.description: Supplement labels should be reviewed for active form, serving size, quality testing, and safety cautions before comparison.

references_subtitle: References should be reviewed by ingredient form, dose, and health context.
